title: Cultural Schism
description: Two decks now keep different calendars. The disagreement has stopped being polite.

outcomes.label: Council mediation
outcomes.description: Spend influence to reconcile the factions.
outcomes.log: The council brokered a settlement between the divided decks.

outcomes.label: Let it run its course
outcomes.description: Cultures drift. Intervening has costs too.
outcomes.log: The schism was left to run its course. One faction would not be reconciled; they took a decommissioned shuttle and their grievances, and departed on their own course.

outcomes.label: Recognize both calendars
outcomes.description: Neither force the decks back together nor let the quarrel fester. Write both calendars into the charter as equal — one hull, two peoples, formally. A pluralism the founders never planned, but a stable one.
outcomes.log: The council did the thing the founders would have found unthinkable: it wrote the disagreement into law, recognizing both calendars, both decks, one charter over two peoples. Unity frayed a little at the seam that would now never fully close — but the ship stopped tearing at it, and a stranger, sturdier thing settled in its place.

complications.description_add: This is no longer a quarrel over calendars — on a ship drifted this far from its founding, the decks scarcely share a language for the disagreement anymore.
complications.log: On a ship so far drifted from its founders, even the reckoning landed in half-shared words, and left the decks a little further apart than it found them.

title: The Skills Drought
description: The last founding specialists are retiring faster than apprentices can replace them. Institutional memory is thinning.

outcomes.label: Crash apprenticeship
outcomes.description: Pull hands from other duties to train replacements now.
outcomes.log: A crash apprenticeship filled the benches, if not yet the shoes.

outcomes.label: Lean on the records
outcomes.description: Trust the archives to carry what the elders knew. Some of it will not translate.
outcomes.log: The crews leaned on the archives. Something in the doing, not the knowing, was lost.

outcomes.label: Bring in outside hands
outcomes.description: Neither grow the skills slowly nor freeze them in records — buy them. Take on trained specialists from a passing ship or a waystation: expertise aboard within the year, at the cost of a founding crew diluted by strangers.
outcomes.log: The ship filled its thinning benches with hired expertise — pilots, medics, machinists who had learned their craft under other stars. The work went on without a gap. But the founding families watched bloodline crews take orders from newcomers, and something of the voyage's inheritance thinned even as its competence held.

complications.description_add: Under the Hearth Union the drought is softened by the table itself — craft passes hand to hand at the common meal the way it always has among them, elders and apprentices seated together, and less is lost than the archivists feared.
complications.log: The Hearth kept the drought from becoming a rout the way it kept everything — at the table, elder beside apprentice, the doing handed over with the bread. What the archives could not carry, the meal did.

title: The Founders' Creed
description: A movement wants the founding charter read aloud each year, word for word, forever. Another wants it revised for a ship the founders never saw.

outcomes.label: Enshrine the charter
outcomes.description: The founders' words become law and liturgy both.
outcomes.log: The charter was enshrined, unaltered. The ship knows exactly who it was meant to be.

outcomes.label: Amend the charter
outcomes.description: A living document for a living ship. Every word changed is a small funeral.
outcomes.log: The charter was amended. The founders would not recognize the ship; the ship is not sure it minds.

title: The Right to Name
description: The destination needs a name for the charts and the histories. Every faction has one ready, and a reason it must win.

outcomes.label: Honor the founders' name
outcomes.description: The name they wrote into the plan, kept.
outcomes.log: The destination kept the name its founders chose. Some traditions are only words until you keep them.

outcomes.label: Let the ship choose
outcomes.description: A vote, deck by deck. Messy, and theirs.
outcomes.log: The ship named its own destination by vote. The charts bear a word no founder wrote.

title: The Last Founders
description: The final three crew who were awake at departure petition the council: they want the mission's original wording read into the record before they die, unamended.

outcomes.label: Honor the petition
outcomes.description: Read the founding words as written. Let the old crew hear them once more.
outcomes.log: The founding words were read whole to the last who remembered them. Some wept; none objected.

outcomes.label: Read the current charter
outcomes.description: The living ship's amended wording is the true text now. Honesty over sentiment.
outcomes.log: The amended charter was read instead. The last founders heard a mission they did not entirely recognize.

title: Born to the Corridors
description: For the first time, the dynasty's leader was born, raised, and will die aboard — with no memory, even inherited, of a world with a sky.

outcomes.label: Mark the moment
outcomes.description: Acknowledge that the ship now leads itself, from itself.
outcomes.log: The ship marked its first wholly ship-born leader. Something ended quietly; something began louder.

title: The Memory Wall
description: The memorial wall has run out of room. The council must decide whether to extend it, archive the oldest names, or begin again.

outcomes.label: Extend the wall
outcomes.description: Give the dead more room, whatever it costs the living decks.
outcomes.log: The memorial wall grew a new wing. No name will be moved while the ship still flies.

outcomes.label: Archive the oldest names
outcomes.description: Move the earliest dead into the record to make room.
outcomes.log: The oldest names went to the archive to make room for the newly gone. The founders moved over, a little.

title: A Voice From Home
description: A tight-beam message from the origin world arrives, decades stale — routine, official, and addressed to a ship the senders assume is still the one that departed.

outcomes.label: Answer home
outcomes.description: Send a reply into a fifty-year silence.
outcomes.log: The ship answered the old signal from home. The reply will arrive to a world that may no longer be listening.

outcomes.label: Log it and move on
outcomes.description: Home is a place none of them will see. Face forward.
outcomes.log: The message from home was logged and archived, unanswered. The ship's home is the ship now.

title: The Last Sky
description: The last surviving photograph of an open planetary sky — the founders' sky — has degraded to almost nothing. The archivists ask whether to restore it, or let it finish fading.

outcomes.label: Restore the image
outcomes.description: Spend to keep the founders' sky.
outcomes.log: The last sky was painstakingly restored. A ship born to corridors keeps one window onto the world it left.

outcomes.label: Let it fade
outcomes.description: Some things are meant to be let go.
outcomes.log: The last photograph of an open sky was allowed to finish fading. The ship's children will never miss what they cannot picture.

title: The Founder's Ledger
description: A founder's private ledger surfaces in the archives — not the official record, but the doubts, the debts, and the compromises behind the mission's clean origin story.

outcomes.label: Publish the ledger
outcomes.description: Let the ship see its founders whole.
outcomes.log: The founder's private ledger was published entire. The founders are smaller now, and truer, and harder to worship.

outcomes.label: Seal the ledger
outcomes.description: Some myths hold a ship together. Let this one stand.
outcomes.log: The ledger was sealed back into the archive. The founders keep their clean story, and the ship keeps the myth it steers by.

title: The Renaming
description: A movement has grown to rename the ship — not for the world it left, which no one aboard remembers, but for the corridors that are the only home its people have known.

outcomes.label: Rename the ship
outcomes.description: Let the ship be named by those who live in it.
outcomes.log: The ship was renamed by the generation that lives in it, over the objections of the archive. The old name is a footnote now, and the corridors answer to their own.

outcomes.label: Keep the founders' name
outcomes.description: The name is a promise to a place. Keep it.
outcomes.log: The founders' name was kept. The ship still carries the word for a world none of its people will ever walk, because the founders asked it to.

title: The Mission Becomes Creed
description: A generation that has never seen a planet has begun to worship the voyage itself — the charter read as scripture, the founders as saints, the destination as a promised land.

outcomes.label: Let the faith carry them
outcomes.description: A ship that believes is a ship that endures.
outcomes.log: The council let the new faith grow. The crew sails on certain of its purpose, and a little further from the people who wrote the charter as a work order.

outcomes.label: Temper it with the record
outcomes.description: Keep the charter a charter, not a gospel.
outcomes.log: The council read the charter aloud as the plain contract it was. Some were steadied; some felt something sacred quietly taken from them.

title: The Silence From Home
description: The long-range relays have gone quiet. Not jammed — quiet. The civilization that launched this ship has stopped answering, and no one aboard remembers its voice well enough to say when.

outcomes.label: Mourn, and go on
outcomes.description: Whatever waits at the end, we are its people now.
outcomes.log: The ship held a wake for a home it could no longer hear. The mission stopped being an errand for someone else and became, simply, the ship's own life.

outcomes.label: Keep the watch
outcomes.description: Someone must still be listening. We keep calling.
outcomes.log: The relays were kept open, calling into a silence that never answered. It is a kind of loyalty, and a kind of grief.

title: The Long Schism
description: The decks that keep different calendars now keep different laws. What began as a cultural drift has hardened into two peoples sharing one hull, and one of them is packing.

outcomes.label: Force the union
outcomes.description: One ship, one law — enforced.
outcomes.log: The council imposed a single law by force of the security decks. The union held. The resentment went underground and stayed there.

outcomes.label: Let the dissenters go
outcomes.description: Better a clean parting than a war in the corridors.
outcomes.log: The dissenting faction took a lifeboat and the ship's blessing and struck out on their own heading. The hull is quieter, and smaller.

title: The Starless Reach
description: For a long stretch the charts show nothing at all — no star near enough to name, no world to steer by. A generation is born and dies seeing only the ship.

outcomes.label: Turn the ship inward
outcomes.description: Make the voyage itself the world. Festivals, archives, invented seasons.
outcomes.log: With no star to steer by, the ship became its own country — new holidays, new songs, a people who called the void home.

outcomes.label: Hold to the mission
outcomes.description: Drill the charter into them. The destination is the meaning.
outcomes.log: The elders held the charter up like a lantern through the starless reach. The people kept faith, and kept their eyes forward, and grew a little grim.

title: Flame and Ascension
description: The augmented and the traditionalists share too few corridors. The Ascension Circle and the Keepers of the First Flame petition, each, for a deck of their own — a soft partition of the ship.

outcomes.label: Grant the separate decks
outcomes.description: Let each keep its own quarter. Peace by distance.
outcomes.log: The ship drew a quiet border down its middle. The corridors grew calmer and the two halves grew stranger to each other.

outcomes.label: Refuse — one ship, shared decks
outcomes.description: No partitions. They will learn to live shoulder to shoulder.
outcomes.log: The council refused the partition and mixed the berth rolls on purpose. The friction stayed, and so, stubbornly, did the sense of one crew.

title: The Stationkeepers
description: Parked at the worksite for lifetimes, a generation comes of age that has never seen the ship move. To them the mission is not a voyage but a place — this orbit, this labor, this sky that never changes.

outcomes.label: Make the work their identity
outcomes.description: Let them be stationkeepers, proud of the labor for its own sake.
outcomes.log: The council blessed the stationkeepers' pride, and a people who had forgotten they were travelers worked the vein like a homeland. Home would be a strange word to them later.

outcomes.label: Keep the voyage alive in them
outcomes.description: Drill the return into every child. This is a stop, not a home.
outcomes.log: The elders held the return before the stationkeepers like a promise, and taught children born to the worksite to ache for a home none of them had seen.

title: The Forge and the Hearth
description: The Steel Covenant wants to strip the old founders' commons — a beloved, inefficient garden-lounge — and replace it with a fabrication bay. The Hearth Union would sooner give up a lung.

outcomes.label: Raise the fabrication bay
outcomes.description: Function wins. The commons becomes a workshop.
outcomes.log: The founders' commons came down and a fabrication bay rose in its place. The ship made more and remembered less, and the Hearth Union mourned a garden as if it were a person.

outcomes.label: Keep the commons
outcomes.description: Some inefficiencies are load-bearing. The garden stays.
outcomes.log: The council spared the founders' commons, and the Covenant built its bay in a cramped hold instead. The garden kept growing, useless and green and beloved.

title: The Teachers' Gap
description: The schools have been coasting — teaching the shape of knowledge without its substance, graduating cohorts who can recite but not reason. One generation more of this and the ship forgets how to teach itself anything.

outcomes.label: Refound the schools
outcomes.description: Pull the best minds from every deck to teach, at a cost to the work.
outcomes.log: The council pulled its finest hands off the line and set them to teaching. Output dipped for a generation and the ship recovered its ability to make more of itself than it had been.

outcomes.label: Let the schools coast
outcomes.description: There is work to do. The children can learn the essentials on the job.
outcomes.log: The schools kept coasting and the cohorts kept reciting. The ship lost, quietly, a little more of its capacity to understand itself — a debt that would come due in some later crisis.

title: The Second Departure
description: The first anomaly is surveyed and logged, and now the ship must do the hardest thing on a double charter — turn away from the direction of home and burn deeper out, toward a second objective a generation of the crew will not live to see completed.

outcomes.label: Hold to the full charter
outcomes.description: Both deeps, as contracted. Burn on toward the second.
outcomes.log: The ship turned its back on the direction of home and burned deeper out, honoring the whole charter. A generation swallowed its longing and flew on toward a horizon it would bequeath to its children.

outcomes.label: Skip the second deep, turn home
outcomes.description: One survey banked is enough. Take the partial pay and go.
outcomes.log: The council banked the first survey and turned for home rather than ask another lifetime of the crew. The pay would be partial; the relief in the corridors was total.

title: The Ascension Exodus
description: Centuries of augmentation have carried the Ascension Circle somewhere the rest of the ship cannot follow. They no longer eat at the common tables, no longer speak the common tongue without effort. Now they ask, gently, to be given a hull of their own and let go.

outcomes.label: Give them a lifeboat and let them go
outcomes.description: Part on good terms. They were always going to leave the human behind.
outcomes.log: The Ascension Circle took a lifeboat and their strange bright future and slipped away into the dark to become whatever they were becoming. The ship felt smaller, and a little more human, for their going.

outcomes.label: Refuse — one ship, one people
outcomes.description: Insist the augmented remain part of the whole, however strained.
outcomes.log: The council refused to let the Circle break away, and the augmented stayed — present, apart, and quietly counting the years until the question was asked again.

title: The Hardened Flame
description: The Keepers of the First Flame have spent generations turning the mission into scripture, and now their faith has teeth — they demand the whole ship keep their rites, or they will keep them alone, apart, behind a sealed bulkhead.

outcomes.label: Bend the ship to their rites
outcomes.description: Adopt the Keepers' observances shipwide to keep them in the fold.
outcomes.log: The ship took up the First Flame's rites to keep the peace, and the mission became, by degrees, a religion the whole crew practiced whether they believed it or not.

outcomes.label: Let them keep to themselves
outcomes.description: Give the orthodox their sealed quarter and let the schism harden.
outcomes.log: The Keepers sealed themselves behind their bulkhead and, over a generation, became a ship-within-the-ship that finally cut its own way loose. The First Flame took its scripture and its silence and left.

title: The Verdant Secession
description: The Verdant Kin have never loved the metal sky, and drift has only deepened their longing for real soil. When the route passes a habitable world, they ask — no, they announce — that they mean to go down and stay, and make a garden of it.

outcomes.label: Bless the planting and let them stay
outcomes.description: Send them down with seed and tools and a benediction.
outcomes.log: The Verdant Kin went down to the green world with seed and song, and the ship sailed on lighter and quieter, having planted a garden it would never see grow.

outcomes.label: Deny them — the mission needs their hands
outcomes.description: The ship cannot spare a whole people. Order them to stay.
outcomes.log: The council forbade the Kin their world, and they stayed — hands on the grow-decks, hearts on a planet dwindling astern. For a generation they tended the gardens like exiles.

title: The Pact Remembered
description: Generations after the lean years, the rationing pact has hardened into the ship's founding virtue — equal shares is not policy now but identity. A proposal to reward exceptional work with extra ration lands like heresy.

outcomes.label: Keep the pact sacred
outcomes.description: Equal shares forever. The pact is who we are.
outcomes.log: The ship reaffirmed the old pact as gospel, and equal shares became a thing beyond questioning. It bound the people tight and left no room for the ambitious, which most of them counted a fair trade.

outcomes.label: Allow earned rewards
outcomes.description: Let merit buy a little more. Loosen the old oath.
outcomes.log: The council let merit earn a larger plate, and the lean pact cracked at last into something more ordinary and less holy. The productive prospered; an old unity quietly dissolved.

title: The Endless Haul
description: A generation has been born, come of age, and taken the line who have known nothing but the tow — the dead ship always behind, home always a century ahead. They ask the elders what the haul is for. The answer will shape who they become.

outcomes.label: Make the haul sacred
outcomes.description: The tow is the ship's great work. Let hauling it be who they are.
outcomes.log: The haul became the ship's religion — the tow-line watch its priesthood, the dead ship behind them a relic they were chosen to bear home. They are one people now, bound to a labor their grandparents took up. What they will do when the haul finally ends, no one has thought to ask.

outcomes.label: Point them at home
outcomes.description: Keep them dreaming of the planetfall none of them will live to see.
outcomes.log: The elders taught the new watch to work for the harbor at the end — a green world in the archives none of them would reach. It kept them steady and it kept them restless, a generation always half-turned toward a home that stayed a century off.

title: The Cold Table
description: The Hearth Union's whole faith is the common table — that the ship eats as one people. But the Ascension Circle no longer eats, not as others do, and the Hearth has begun to wonder aloud what a table is for when a third of the ship will not sit at it.

outcomes.label: Set a place for those who cannot eat
outcomes.description: Spend the influence to keep the common table common — a seat kept even for the augmented who only sit, and do not eat.
outcomes.log: The Hearth kept its table whole, laying a place for the Circle who came, and sat, and did not eat, and were counted anyway. It cost the council to insist on it, and it held the ship together the way the table always had.

outcomes.label: Let the Hearth keep its own table
outcomes.description: Let the Union take a hull where the old rite still means something and everyone at the table still eats.
outcomes.log: The Hearth Union settled a warm green world and kept its table there, among people who all still ate at it. The ship that sailed on was more efficient and colder for their going — no one now whose whole calling was to make the ship feel like a home.

title: The Crumbling Archive
description: It is not that the teachers have forgotten — it is that the archive itself is dying. The data-looms fail, the record substrate flakes and greys, whole shelves of the founding library reading back as noise. The ship's memory is rotting on its racks.

outcomes.label: Mount the great copying
outcomes.description: Set a generation to hand-copying the failing archive onto fresh media before it is lost. A vast, patient labor.
outcomes.log: A whole cohort gave its youth to the copying — reading the greying archive aloud and writing it fresh, shelf by failing shelf, before the noise took it. The library will outlive them now, in a hand not its own, and the ship kept its memory whole.

outcomes.label: Let the oldest records go
outcomes.description: Save what is used; let the deep archive — the parts no one has opened in lifetimes — fade.
outcomes.log: The council saved the working records and let the deep archive go — the founding debates, the old star-charts, the names of worlds no one was steering for anymore. The ship reads lighter now, and remembers less of why it ever left.

title: The Only World
description: A census officer brings the council a quiet finding: not one soul now aboard was alive at launch, and by the arithmetic of the voyage, not one now living expects to see the end. For everyone who breathes, the ship is not the way to the world. The ship is the world.

outcomes.label: Teach the voyage as a passage
outcomes.description: Hold the founding and the far harbor alive in every school. The ship is a corridor between two worlds, and these are the people of the crossing.
outcomes.log: The schools kept teaching the ship as a passage — a bridge thrown between a world left and a world promised, and every child a walker on it. It gave the voyage a spine and a sadness both: a people forever between, at home in neither dark they came from nor light they steer for.

outcomes.label: Teach the ship as home
outcomes.description: Let them have the ship as the whole and only world it has become for them. The mission can be an inheritance without being a homesickness.
outcomes.log: The council let the schools teach the ship as the world entire — not a corridor but a country, with its own weather of light-cycles and its own horizon of bulkheads. The people grew happy in it and strange to their charter, the far harbor fading into a story told at festivals, meaning less each time.

title: The Far Horizon
description: A generation has done the arithmetic that the charter tried to keep gentle: the ship is still outbound, still burning toward a target none of them will ever see resolve, and the survey their grandparents promised belongs to grandchildren not yet born. They are the crossing generation — all cost, no arrival. They want to know what they are for.

outcomes.label: Hold the heading, honor the crossing
outcomes.description: Teach the crossing itself as the work — that carrying the ship one more generation toward the horizon is a life's worth of purpose, arrival or no.
outcomes.log: The ship taught its crossing generation to take pride in the burn itself — in being the hands that carried her another lifetime toward a horizon they would never reach. It held them to the heading, and it asked of them a patience that is close kin to faith.

outcomes.label: Let the crossing become the point
outcomes.description: Stop pretending the destination is theirs. Let this generation build a life for its own sake in the long burn, target or no target.
outcomes.log: The council stopped asking the crossing generation to live for a horizon and let them live for the ship they were on. They grew content and a little untethered from the writ, and the far target became a thing the ship was pointed at rather than a thing anyone still ached to reach.

title: The Green Hearth
description: Generations of shared work have blurred the old line between the Hearth Union and the Verdant Kin — the ones who keep the table and the ones who keep the garden. Their children intermarry, their festivals have merged, their councils sit together more often than apart. Now the two peoples ask the ship to recognize what has already happened: they wish to become one.

outcomes.label: Bless the union
outcomes.description: Let the table and the garden become one people. Two founding names become one living tradition.
outcomes.log: The Hearth and the Kin were joined by the ship's blessing, and where two peoples had kept the ship's warmth and the ship's green between them, now one people kept both. The founders' careful roster is one name shorter, and the ship is a warmer, less varied place for it.

outcomes.label: Keep the two names
outcomes.description: The founders set these peoples apart for a reason. Honor the old distinction, whatever the young are doing.
outcomes.log: The council held the two names apart as the founders drew them, and the Hearth and the Kin stayed, on the roster at least, two peoples. Their children went on marrying across the line and keeping one another's festivals, and calling the council's distinction a thing for the council.

title: The Technocrats' Compact
description: The Meridian Accord runs the ship's courts and the Steel Covenant runs its workshops, and after enough generations of running the ship's institutions shoulder to shoulder, the two have proposed something the founders never drew: a single administration, courts and forges under one hand, the argument and the labor finally in the same room.

outcomes.label: Form the compact
outcomes.description: One administration, courts and forges together. Efficient, unified, and a concentration of power the founders spread on purpose.
outcomes.log: The Accord and the Covenant folded into a single administration, and the ship ran, for a while, with a smoothness it had not known — the courts and the workshops speaking at last with one voice. The founders had kept those voices separate on purpose, and no one now aboard remembered quite why.

outcomes.label: Keep the powers apart
outcomes.description: Courts that judge the makers and makers who build for the courts — the founders split these on purpose. Refuse the merger.
outcomes.log: The council refused the compact and kept the courts and the forges as separate powers, each a check on the other, the way the founding charter drew them. It was less efficient and more free, and the two institutions went on eyeing each other across a line the founders had meant them to keep.

title: The Two Ships
description: The ship has come apart without a single wall being built. The crew keep separate corridors now, separate mess halls, separate words for the same things; a child raised on one side of the divide can go a lifetime barely speaking to the other. It is one hull and, by every measure that matters, two peoples.

outcomes.label: Force them back together
outcomes.description: Spend the council's whole authority on knocking down the invisible walls — shared shifts, shared tables, shared schools, whether they like it or not.
outcomes.log: The council forced the two ships back into one by main strength — mixing the shifts, the tables, the classrooms, over the sullen resistance of people who had grown comfortable apart. It held, barely, and it cost the council every favor it had, and the ship was one people again the way a set bone is one bone.

outcomes.label: Let them be two under one charter
outcomes.description: Stop pretending. Recognize the two communities formally, bind them only to the shared mission, and let each keep its own life.
outcomes.log: The council wrote the division into the charter rather than fight it — two communities, one hull, one destination, and a careful peace between. The ship ran smoother for the honesty and sailed on as two peoples who had agreed on exactly one thing, which was where they were going.

title: The Broken Council
description: The council itself has fractured. The blocs will not sit in the same room; sessions collapse into walkouts; a decision that once took a week now takes a decade or never comes at all. The ship is drifting not because no one is steering but because the steering has come apart in too many hands.

outcomes.label: Consolidate under one hand
outcomes.description: Break the deadlock by breaking the council — vest authority in a single office that can actually decide. The ship will move again. It will move less like the founders meant.
outcomes.log: The deadlock was broken by ending the thing that deadlocked: one office now, one hand on the helm, and the ship deciding again with a speed it had forgotten. The founders had built a council on purpose, against exactly this. No one who remembered why was still aboard to say so.

outcomes.label: Let the decks rule themselves
outcomes.description: If the center cannot hold, let it go. Devolve real power to the decks — local rule, local custom — and keep only the mission in common.
outcomes.log: The council let the center dissolve and gave the decks their own rule, and the ship became a loose confederation of corridors that shared a hull and a heading and less and less else. People were happier close to home and stranger to one another, and the common archive thinned as each deck kept only its own.

title: Founders' Day
description: The anniversary of Launch comes round again, and the ship must decide, one more time, how to keep it. To the elders it is the most solemn day of the year — the day the whole enterprise began. To the young it is a day off from the rota with speeches attached.

outcomes.label: Keep the solemn rite
outcomes.description: The full observance — the founding log read aloud, the names of the first crew spoken, the charter renewed. A day to remember why.
outcomes.log: Founders' Day was kept in full — the log read out end to end, the first names spoken into the recyclers' hum, the charter renewed by a show of hands. The ship remembered, for one day, exactly what it was and why, and stood a little straighter for it into the year.

outcomes.label: Let it lighten
outcomes.description: The young want a festival, not a funeral for people they never met. Let Founders' Day become a celebration on its own terms.
outcomes.log: Founders' Day loosened into a proper festival — music the founders never heard, games they never played, and only a short reading no one much attended. The ship enjoyed itself thoroughly and remembered the founders the way one remembers a distant, well-meaning ancestor: fondly, and not in much detail.

title: The Half-Remembered Founding
description: The holiday is still kept — the ship has always kept it — but no one alive can quite say what it is for. The founding log is a thing the schools reference and no one reads; the first names on the roll are syllables in a song. It is a good festival. It has simply come loose from its reason.

outcomes.label: Teach the founding again
outcomes.description: Mount an effort to reconnect the festival to its history — the log taught, the names learned, the reason spoken. Row against the drift of centuries.
outcomes.log: The council spent its authority teaching the ship its own festival again — the log, the names, the reason — and for a while the holiday meant something once more, though the young kept the old songs mostly for the tunes. You can teach a people their history. You cannot make them ache for it.

outcomes.label: Let the holiday stand on its own
outcomes.description: It is a good festival and the ship loves it. Let it be what it has become — a thing the ship does because the ship has always done it, meaning whatever the living decide it means.
outcomes.log: The council let the holiday be what it had become — untethered from its founding, kept for its own warm sake, a tradition whose only remaining reason was that the ship had always kept it. Which is, in the end, the reason for most traditions, and the founders, whatever they were, had wanted the ship to go on.

title: The Keepers' Petition
description: The Keepers of the First Flame come before the council with a grievance dressed as a request: the reactor-shrine's eternal burn, they say, has been treated as a machine to be throttled when the grow-decks want power, and a faith cannot be dimmed at a quartermaster's convenience. They ask that the flame be given standing that no ration board can override.

outcomes.label: Grant the flame its standing
outcomes.description: Write the eternal burn into the ship's law above the ration board. The Keepers are honoured — and the grow-decks and industry learn to make do with what the shrine leaves them.
outcomes.log: The council gave the First Flame's shrine standing above the ration board, and the Keepers bowed to a ship that understood a faith is not a furnace to be banked. The grow-decks ran a little dimmer for it, and the Keepers, for a while, counted themselves a people heard.

outcomes.label: The ship's needs come first
outcomes.description: A voyage runs on power budgets, not creeds, and the shrine will take its share like every other draw when the ship is short. Say so plainly, whatever the Keepers make of it.
outcomes.log: The council told the Keepers plainly that the shrine would be throttled with everything else when the ship ran short, faith or no faith. It was true, and it was well-run, and the Keepers of the First Flame went back to their decks having learned exactly where their fire stood on this ship's ledger. They did not forget it.

title: The Flame Withdraws
description: The Keepers of the First Flame have stopped petitioning. Slighted one time too many on a ship that has made plain, over generations, that their faith is a line item, they have quietly gathered their people and their portable braziers and asked for a berth's worth of the ship — a shuttle, a share of stores, and leave to go find a world that will let their fire burn without a quartermaster's leave.

outcomes.label: Release them with honour
outcomes.description: A people held against their will is no people at all. Give the Keepers their share and a proper farewell, and let the ship sail on without the fire it could not keep.
outcomes.log: The ship gave the Keepers of the First Flame their berth and their stores and a farewell with the honours due a founding people, and the eternal flame went with them into the dark to find a world that would keep it. The ship sailed on lighter and colder, and those who remained understood that a people slighted long enough will, in the end, simply leave.

outcomes.label: Pay any price to keep them
outcomes.description: Losing a founding people is a wound the voyage may not survive. Give the Keepers everything they asked for and more — the shrine's standing, a public penance, a seat on the council — and win them back at whatever it costs.
outcomes.log: The council did what pride had refused for generations: it went to the Keepers with a penance and a place at the table and the shrine's standing written above the ration board at last, and it paid for the reconciliation in power and standing both. The First Flame stayed, mollified and watchful, and the ship kept its founding fire — having learned, expensively, that goodwill spent is dearer to buy back than it ever was to keep.

title: The Sealed Capsule
description: A founder proposes something the ship has time enough for and nothing else quite like: a sealed capsule — letters, warnings, hopes, the plain truth of who launched this ship and why — set to be opened by hands not yet born, at a fixed remove of fifty years. A message from the people who cast off to the people who will have forgotten them, timed to arrive when it will land as a stranger's voice.

outcomes.label: Seal it, to open in fifty years
outcomes.description: Fill the capsule with the founding truth and lock it to a date two generations out. Trust the descendants to want it, and trust the present to have something worth saying to a future it will not see.
outcomes.log: The founders filled a capsule with the truth of themselves — the fear and the hope and the names — and sealed it to a date fifty years on, a message fired forward through time to hands not yet born. Then they went back to the work, having spoken, once, plainly, to a future that would have to decide for itself what to make of them.

outcomes.label: Let the future be its own
outcomes.description: A people should not be governed by voices in a box. Say what needs saying now, to the living, and let those who come after write their own founding without the founders leaning over their shoulder.
outcomes.log: The founders declined to bottle themselves for posterity, and said what they had to say to the living and let it go at that. The future would have to invent its own account of where it came from — which, some argued, is the only kind of founding a people ever really keeps.

title: The Capsule Opens
description: The date the founders set has come. A capsule none of the living sealed, addressed to none of the living by name, unlocks itself on schedule and offers up the voices of people fifty years dead — their fears for a ship that has since weathered worse and stranger, their hopes for descendants who have become someone the senders would not quite recognize. The founding, speaking across the gap it always knew would open.

outcomes.label: Take the founders at their word
outcomes.description: They saw this far and spoke to it; the least the ship can do is listen and be bound. Read the capsule into the record as instruction, and let the dead have their say in the living ship.
outcomes.log: The ship took its founders at their word across fifty years, read the capsule into the charter as instruction, and let the dead steer a little of the living ship. Something in the voyage straightened toward its origin, and something else — the right of the present to decide for itself — bent a degree to make room for the voices in the box.

outcomes.label: Thank them, and decide for yourselves
outcomes.description: Hear the founders out with all the honour they are owed — and then remember that they are dead, and the ship is not, and the people who must live the next fifty years are the ones who should choose them. Keep the capsule as history, not as law.
outcomes.log: The ship heard its founders out across the gap of fifty years, thanked them with full honours, and then did what the living do: decided for itself. The capsule went into the archive as history rather than law, one more voice the ship could weigh and need not obey — which was, the record noted, most likely what the wiser founders had hoped a free people would do with them.

title: The Work Half Done
description: A quiet arithmetic reaches the council: the charter's tally has passed its midpoint. Half the cores are cut, half the systems charted, half the accord kept — and no one aboard who began the work will be aboard to finish it. A generation that inherited a purpose it did not choose has carried it to the halfway mark, and pauses, for a breath, to ask what it is carrying.

outcomes.label: Rededicate to the founders' charter
outcomes.description: The purpose is the point; the ship exists to keep it. Read the founding charter aloud at the midpoint, renew the vow, and carry the work the rest of the way in the founders' name.
outcomes.log: The ship marked the halfway tally by reading the founders' charter aloud and renewing the vow, and a generation that had never chosen the work chose it, freely, at the midpoint. The purpose held, and steadied everything around it — a people is rarely stronger than when it decides that the thing it was handed is a thing it wants.

outcomes.label: Ask whether the purpose is still theirs
outcomes.description: Honour the work, but say the quiet thing plainly: this is the founders' mission, not this generation's, and a people has the right to weigh a purpose it did not set before spending the rest of its life on it. Let the question be asked in the open.
outcomes.log: The ship let the quiet question be asked in daylight at the halfway mark — whether a purpose handed down is a purpose owed — and something shifted that would not shift back. The work went on, but carried now by a people who had admitted, aloud, that it was theirs to weigh and not only to obey. The founders would have called it drift. The living called it growing up.

title: The Charter Fulfilled
description: The tally is complete. The holds are full, the survey closed, the accord signed — whatever the founders sent this ship three centuries to do is, as of this watch, done. And the ship is not home. The long return still lies ahead, years or generations of it, and for the first time the voyage faces the strange question of what a ship is for once its reason for being is behind it.

outcomes.label: Mark it as the founders' triumph
outcomes.description: This is the moment the whole voyage was launched to reach. Strike it into the record as the founders' victory, teach the children that the ship kept a promise older than any of them, and let the completed charter be the ship's proudest fact all the way home.
outcomes.log: The ship marked the charter's completion as the founders' triumph, struck it into the record, and taught it to the children as the proudest fact of the voyage: that a promise three centuries old had been kept by hands that never met the ones who made it. The long way home would be flown by a people who knew, past any doubt, that they had done the thing they were for.

outcomes.label: Face the ship with no more purpose
outcomes.description: The charter is done and the truth is plain: the mission that defined every soul aboard is now behind them, and the years of return ahead have no purpose written into them at all. Let the ship sit with that — a people that must, for the first time, decide for itself what it is for.
outcomes.log: The ship finished its charter and let itself feel the strange hollow after — the mission that had shaped every life aboard now behind them, the long return ahead an open page. Some drifted; some invented new reasons; all of them understood, for the first time, that a purpose fulfilled is a purpose that no longer holds you, and that what a people does with its freedom is a harder question than what it does with its orders.

title: The Homeward Face
description: The ship has turned for home, and a generation that has known only the outbound dark does the arithmetic of arrival: the homeport its charts remember will remember the founders — their speech, their calendar, their faces — and will be waiting to greet those founders in the crew that comes back. But the crew that comes back is not those founders, and has not been for a long time. The question of what face to wear into homeport cannot be put off much longer.

outcomes.label: Arrive as the founders' faithful
outcomes.description: Home does not need to know how far the voyage drifted. Rehearse the old speech, restore the old calendar for the arrival, present the ship home wanted to see — the founders' errand faithfully run by the founders' faithful children. Give the homeport its homecoming, whatever it costs the truth.
outcomes.log: The ship drilled itself back toward the founders' ways for the length of the return — the old speech, the old calendar, the homecoming the homeport was waiting for — and resolved to arrive as the faithful children of the crew that left. It was a kindness and a performance both, a whole generation learning to wear a face that had not been the ship's true face in a century, so that home could have the reunion it had kept a light burning for.

outcomes.label: Arrive as who the voyage made us
outcomes.description: The people who come home are the people the voyage made, and they will not land wearing a mask of the dead to spare the living a surprise. Meet the homeport honestly — changed speech, changed ways, changed faces — and let home meet the crew that actually flew the dark, not the ghost it expected.
outcomes.log: The ship decided it would land as itself and not as its founders' ghost — the drifted speech, the invented holidays, the faces a century of the dark had made — and let the homeport meet the people who had actually flown it rather than the ones it had waved off. Some of the crew feared the welcome would be colder for the honesty. Most judged that a home worth coming back to could bear to learn who had come back, and that a reunion built on a mask is a reunion with no one at all.

title: The Stranger Homeport
description: The return course brings the latest word of home with it, and the word is strange: the homeport the founders left is centuries gone about its own business, changed in ways no one aboard has lived to watch — new powers, new tongues, a memory of the voyage grown thin and half-legendary. The ship is not returning to the world it departed. Both ends of the journey have drifted, and only now, turning for home, does the crew reckon with a homecoming to a place as much a stranger as they are.

outcomes.label: Hold to the home the founders carried
outcomes.description: The homeport in the founding logs is the one the voyage was sent from and sworn to; keep faith with that home, teach the children the world the founders knew, and let the arrival sort out the difference between the remembered place and the real one.
outcomes.log: The ship kept faith with the home its founders had carried in the logs — the streets and the songs and the promises of a place centuries gone — and taught its children to come home to that. It was a loyalty and a setup both: a people sailing toward a memory, who would meet the real homeport, whatever it had become, with the founders' expectations and the founders' certainty, and learn the difference the hard way, at the dock.

outcomes.label: Accept that home has changed too
outcomes.description: The founders' homeport is as dead as the founders. Whatever waits at the dock is a new place that half-remembers an old errand, and the ship will meet it as it is — no grievance that it changed, no pretence that it didn't — two strangers who happen to share a name and a story, deciding what that is worth now.
outcomes.log: The ship let go of the homeport in the logs and made its peace with meeting whatever now held that name — a changed place greeting a changed crew, two strangers with a shared story and no living witness to it between them. It was a colder homecoming than the founders had promised and a truer one than they could have imagined: not a return to anything, but a meeting, between a voyage and a world that had each become someone else while the other was away.

title: The Shifting Helm
description: The census makes it plain what the decades made slowly true: the people who now fill the most berths, who sit the most council seats, whose children crowd the schools, are not the people who did so at launch. The balance has tipped over the generations, quietly, cradle by cradle, and with it the hands on the ship's helm. A people that boarded a minority sets the course now, and must decide how to steer a ship built in another's image.

outcomes.label: Steer the ship as it was built to be steered
outcomes.description: A majority is not a mandate to remake everything. Take the helm and keep the ship's inherited course — the founding charter, the old offices, the ways that worked for the people who are now the fewer — and let the change of hands be a change of hands and nothing more.
outcomes.log: The new majority took the helm and steered by the old stars — the founding charter kept, the inherited offices honoured, the ship run as it had always been run though different hands now ran it. It was a generous restraint and a slightly airless one, a people declining to remake a ship into their own image out of respect for the image it already wore. The continuity held. Whether it was the new majority's ship or only the old majority's ship in new hands was a question the council carefully did not put.

outcomes.label: Make it the ship of the people who fill it
outcomes.description: A ship belongs to the people who live it, not the ones who drew it. Reshape the offices, the calendar, the assumptions to fit the majority that actually crews the decks now — not out of grievance, but because a home should look like the people in it.
outcomes.log: The new majority remade the ship in its own image — the offices reshaped, the calendar redrawn, the founding assumptions quietly retired in favour of the ones the living actually held — not in anger but in the plain conviction that a home should look like the people who live in it. The ship that resulted was warmer to its majority and stranger to its founders, and drifted a further degree from the thing that had left home, which the new majority counted not a loss but a coming-of-age.

title: The Inherited Ship
description: A young officer of the ship's new majority walks the founder decks and feels the strangeness of them: the corridors laid out to another people's habits, the shrines to another people's dead, the whole architecture of the voyage shaped by assumptions its makers never thought to question and its inheritors no longer share. The ship was built by and for a people this generation is no longer chiefly made of. The question of what to do with an inheritance you did not choose comes, as it always does, to the council.

outcomes.label: Keep faith with the ship as built
outcomes.description: The founders built well, for reasons that held, and a people that tears out an inheritance it does not understand often finds it needed the parts it threw away. Live in the ship as given, learn why it was made this way, and change nothing that is not broken.
outcomes.log: The council chose to keep faith with the ship as the founders built it — to live in the inherited architecture, learn the reasons behind its stranger choices, and change nothing merely because it was strange. It was a humility toward the dead that the dead had not earned and the living freely gave, and it kept the ship a coherent thing across the change of its people, an inheritance carried rather than gutted.

outcomes.label: Reshape the ship for who lives here now
outcomes.description: Reverence for a build is not the same as a working home. Rework the decks, the shrines, the assumptions for the people who actually crew them — keep what serves, retire what only ever served the founders, and let the ship become an honest reflection of its living majority.
outcomes.log: The council reworked the inherited ship for the people who lived in it now — the founder decks re-laid, the old shrines kept or quietly closed by whether anyone still prayed at them, the founding assumptions weighed on their merits rather than their pedigree. What came out the far side was less a monument and more a home, honest about its living majority and a further step from its founders, which the young officers who had felt the strangeness counted exactly right.

title: The Founders Vindicated
description: Generations on, a council sits with the whole founding record open before it and weighs the choices the ship has argued over for centuries — the ones that looked, from close up, like cruelty or folly. With the long view and the evidence entire, the verdict comes back the other way: the founders were right, and provably so, and the ship owes them an apology it can no longer deliver. It is a vindication earned by proof, not asked on faith.

outcomes.label: Let the vindication renew the bond
outcomes.description: Carry the verdict to every deck. A people that has just proven its founders wise on the evidence has been handed something rare — a reason, not a rule, to hold the founding close — and a ship should not waste a loyalty that has been earned rather than merely inherited.
outcomes.log: The council carried the vindication to every deck, and a founding that had been an inheritance became, for a generation, a conviction — the founders held close not from duty but because the record had proven them right. It was the rare loyalty that argument strengthens rather than strains, and the ship sailed on the surer for having questioned and been answered.

outcomes.label: Note it, but keep the ship's own counsel
outcomes.description: The founders were right this time, and the record says so, and that is worth knowing — but a living ship steers by its own judgment, not by the posthumous scores of the dead. Honor the verdict, file it, and keep the habit of deciding for yourselves.
outcomes.log: The council noted the founders' vindication, honored it, and filed it — and went on steering by its own lights, unwilling to let even a proven-wise founding become a hand on the helm from beyond the grave. It was a cooler gratitude, and a mature one, the living crediting the dead without kneeling to them.

title: The Mending
description: There was a season not long past when the ship came apart — the councils deadlocked, the decks drawn into camps, the founding charter invoked by every faction against every other until it meant nothing. And then, slowly and without any single reason anyone can name, it healed. The quarrels wore themselves out, the shared work reasserted itself, and a generation that grew up amid the fracture finds itself, to its own surprise, whole. A ship that has just remembered how to be one ship must decide what to make of the memory.

outcomes.label: Enshrine the reconciliation
outcomes.description: Name the mending, mark it, teach it — a day of it, a rite, a story every child learns — so that the next time the ship starts to come apart, it will remember that it came back together once, and can again. A people that knows it has healed is harder to break.
outcomes.log: The council made the mending into memory — a rite, a told story, a thing the ship now knows about itself — so that the reconciliation became not merely a season survived but a proof kept: that this crew, whatever it is now, can come apart and find its way back. It was a wise use of a hard-won peace, an inoculation against the next fracture written into the ship's own idea of itself.

outcomes.label: Let it pass unmarked, and just be whole
outcomes.description: Do not pick at it. The ship healed; naming the wound too loudly can reopen it, and a people that made itself one again out of plain exhaustion with being two does not necessarily want a monument to the quarrel. Let the peace be ordinary, and let ordinary be enough.
outcomes.log: The council chose not to enshrine the mending but simply to live in it, letting the reconciliation be an ordinary quiet rather than a marked triumph. It left the ship a little less armored against the next fracture and a little more at ease in this peace, a people declining to make a monument of a wound it would rather let close and forget.

title: The Forged Unity
description: The old hands say a ship is never so united as just after it has nearly torn itself in half. The crew that has climbed back from a true fracture is a different thing from one that never fell — it has *tested* its bonds and found they hold, and there is a hard confidence in it, a we-have-been-through-worse that the untroubled ship can only envy. A council weighs what to do with a cohesion forged rather than inherited.

outcomes.label: Spend the hard confidence on something bold
outcomes.description: A crew this sure of itself can attempt what a fragile one cannot — a hard course, a great work, a risk the ship would never have dared while it doubted its own seams. Put the forged unity to use before the ease of good years softens it back into the ordinary kind.
outcomes.log: The council spent the ship's hard-forged confidence on something worthy of it — a bold course a fragile crew would never have risked — trusting bonds that had already survived a fracture to survive an ambition. It moved the ship a further step from the founders' caution and toward a self-assurance the founders never had the chance to earn, the particular boldness of a people that has already been to the brink and back.

outcomes.label: Bank it as hard-won wisdom
outcomes.description: Do not spend the confidence chasing greatness; keep it as ballast. A ship that remembers exactly how close it came to breaking, and exactly what pulled it back, holds a steadiness worth more than any bold stroke — the sober wisdom of the once-fractured, kept as a permanent hand on the tiller.
outcomes.log: The council banked the forged unity as wisdom rather than spending it as daring — the ship keeping, as permanent ballast, the sober memory of how near it had come and what had saved it. It was the steadier choice and the less glorious one, a people that had been to the brink deciding that the chief use of the return was never to go there again.

title: The Lapsed Covenant
description: It is not that the ship has forgotten the founders — their names are still taught, their images still hang in the council hall. It is that a generation has quietly stopped obeying them. The founding charter, once read as law, is invoked now the way one invokes an old and respected relative's opinion: heard, weighed, and set aside when it does not suit. No one voted to unbind the covenant. It simply lapsed, the way authority does when enough people stop pretending to feel it, and now the council must decide whether to admit as much aloud.

outcomes.label: Say it plainly: the charter is ours to amend
outcomes.description: Stop pretending. Declare openly what has been true in practice for a generation — that the founding charter is a foundation and not a cage, that the living govern themselves and consult the founders rather than obey them. Better an honest self-rule than a reverence everyone privately ignores.
outcomes.log: The council said aloud what the decks had long since decided in silence: that the founding charter was theirs to amend, a foundation and not a cage, and that the living would govern themselves and consult the dead rather than kneel to them. It was an honest end to a reverence already hollow, and it steadied the ship's governance even as it cut the last formal cord to the founders — a people finally ruling in its own name.

outcomes.label: Renew the covenant before it dies
outcomes.description: A charter unbound is a ship unmoored. Before the founding authority lapses past recovery, renew it deliberately — re-swear the covenant, teach again why the founders bound their heirs as they did, and choose to be bound because the binding has kept the ship whole across the centuries the founders foresaw and the living forget.
outcomes.log: The council chose to renew the fading covenant rather than let it lapse — re-swearing the founders' charter deliberately, teaching a doubting generation why the dead had bound their heirs, and electing to be bound because the binding had carried the ship this far. It was a conservative's victory and a fragile one, authority propped up by will where it had once stood on its own, but the founders' hand was back on the tiller for another generation at least.

title: The Advisory Founders
description: A dispute reaches the council, and someone does the thing that would once have ended the argument: they read the relevant founding decree aloud. And the room simply... continues. The decree is noted, thanked, and worked around, because the generation deciding this has never once felt the founders as a living authority — only as history, wise perhaps, but no more binding than any old and clever text. The founders have become advisory, and the ship must decide how to feel about that.

outcomes.label: Rule by the living's judgment
outcomes.description: The founders were brilliant and they are dead, and they did not know this world, this crew, this century — the living do. Let the founding decrees advise and let the present decide, trusting the generation that actually has to live with a choice over the one that only imagined it.
outcomes.log: The council took up the mandate of the living, letting the founders advise and the present decide, on the plain conviction that the generation which must live with a choice knows more than the one that only foresaw it. It was a confident, forward-facing self-government, and it moved the ship another degree from the founders toward whoever it was now becoming, trusting its own judgment as the founders had trusted theirs.

outcomes.label: Fear a ship that answers to no one
outcomes.description: A crew that heeds no authority above its own passing mood is one bad generation from disaster — the founders bound their heirs precisely because they knew a voyage this long would tempt every generation to remake the rules to suit itself. Restore the decrees' weight, not from reverence but from prudence: some hand must be on the tiller that the living cannot simply vote away.
outcomes.log: The council restored the founding decrees' binding weight — not from reverence, which had long since thinned, but from a cold prudence: that a crew answering only to its own mood was a bad generation away from ruin, and that the founders had bound their heirs precisely because they foresaw the temptation the living felt. It was authority chosen as ballast rather than felt as faith, a hand kept on the tiller that no single generation could vote away.

title: The Ungoverned Ship
description: The ship is not at war with itself; the people, on the whole, get along. It is simply that the machinery of governing them has quietly stopped working. The council rarely reaches quorum and rarer still agrees on anything; half the founding offices sit unfilled, their duties done by whoever happens to be willing; the charter's rules are honored the way old customs are, when convenient and not otherwise. The ship runs on habit, goodwill, and the memory of order — which serves, right up until the day it is asked to do something habit cannot.

outcomes.label: Rebuild the institutions in earnest
outcomes.description: Habit is not government. Re-establish the council with real quorum and real teeth, fill the founding offices and hold their holders to account, enforce the charter as law and not as folklore — accept that a people used to informal ease will chafe at the return of formality, and rebuild the structures anyway, because a ship that governs by goodwill has no answer ready for the day goodwill runs out.
outcomes.log: The council rebuilt itself into something worth the name — quorum enforced, offices filled and answerable, the charter restored from custom to law. The ship grumbled at the return of formality it had grown out of the habit of, and it grumbled from behind functioning institutions for the first time in a generation, a polity again and not merely a crowd that mostly got along.

outcomes.label: Govern by habit and consensus
outcomes.description: The founders' institutions were built for a ship that no longer exists. Stop pretending to a formal order no one uses and make the informal one honest: rule by the standing consensus of the decks, by the people who actually do the work, by custom openly acknowledged as the real law. It is fragile against a true crisis, but it is what the ship is, and a government a people actually uses beats a grand one it ignores.
outcomes.log: The ship stopped pretending to the founders' formal order and made its informal one honest — government by the open consensus of the decks, custom acknowledged as the real law, the empty offices quietly retired rather than mournfully preserved. It was a lighter and a more fragile way to be ruled, fitted to the ship the voyage had actually become, and everyone knew it would hold only until the day it was asked to bear a weight consensus could not.

title: The Hollow Offices
description: A crisis, a small one, needs a decision made and made bindingly — and the ship discovers it no longer has anyone empowered to make it. The offices that once held that authority are hollow forms, their titles worn by people who never truly held the power the titles imply, because the power drained out of the institutions so gradually that no one noticed the moment it was gone. The ship must reckon with a government that exists entirely on paper.

outcomes.label: Put the power back in the offices
outcomes.description: A title with no authority behind it is worse than no title — it is a promise of order the ship cannot keep. Reforge the offices with real power, whatever the discomfort of concentrating authority again, so that when the ship needs a binding decision it has someone who can bind it. Better a government feared a little than one that cannot govern at all.
outcomes.log: The ship poured authority back into its hollow offices, reforging titles into powers a people had grown unused to obeying. It was an uncomfortable reconcentration of a thing the drift had gently dispersed, and it left the ship able, once more, to make a decision and mean it — a government that could govern, feared a little and functional again.

outcomes.label: Improvise it, this time and every time
outcomes.description: There is no time to rebuild a government before this decision must be made, so make it the way the ship makes everything now: in the moment, by whoever is present and willing, by a rough consensus hammered out under pressure. It works this time. It has worked every time so far. Trust that it will keep working, and let the offices stay the hollow forms they have become.
outcomes.log: The ship met the crisis the way it met everything now — improvised in the moment by whoever was present and willing, a rough consensus hammered out under pressure and, this time, sufficient. The hollow offices stayed hollow, the ship trusting its knack for muddling through over any structure it might rebuild, and it muddled through, one more time, on the strength of that trust and nothing firmer.

title: The Name We Carry
description: A generation grows up learning that the ship is famous — that its name, out in the trade-dark it will never see, means kindness. Strangers trust it on sight; the desperate steer for it; other hulls speak of it the way one speaks of the rare good place. It is a lovely thing to inherit and a heavy one, because a reputation for mercy is not a possession but a promise, made by the dead and the living both, that this ship will keep being the thing everyone believes it is. The council must decide what to do with a name it did not choose but must now live up to.

outcomes.label: Make the name a creed to live by
outcomes.description: A reputation this good is a gift and a discipline: teach the young that the ship's mercy is not luck but law, a standard every generation is bound to meet, so that the name means the same in a hundred years as it does now. Bind the ship to its own best reputation, and let the promise the name makes be a promise the ship always keeps.
outcomes.log: The council made the ship's good name into a creed — mercy taught not as fortune but as obligation, a standard the young were bound to meet so the name would mean the same for their grandchildren as it did for them. It was a lovely burden to lay on a people and a real one, a ship choosing to be held to its own best reputation, and the trade-dark went on trusting a hull that had decided, deliberately, to deserve it.

outcomes.label: Refuse to be imprisoned by a reputation
outcomes.description: A name is a cage as much as a crown. A ship bound to be merciful in all things, always, has surrendered its judgment to strangers' expectations — and there will come a day when the merciful choice is the wrong one, and the ship must be free to make it. Honor the reputation without kneeling to it; keep the freedom to be, sometimes, other than kind.
outcomes.log: The council declined to be imprisoned by its own good name, honoring the reputation without kneeling to it and keeping the freedom to be, when a hard day demanded, other than kind. It cost the ship a little of the shine strangers saw in it, and it kept something the pedestal would have taken: the plain right of a living people to judge each case on its merits rather than perform a mercy the dead had promised on its behalf.

title: The Name We Earned
description: There is no avoiding it any longer: the ship has a reputation, and the reputation is fear. Out in the dark it will never see, its name means the hard thing done without flinching — the stores taken and the castaways left, the weak cut to spare the strong, the cold sum always chosen. A generation that made few of those choices itself inherits the sum of them, and must look squarely at what the ship has become, one unflinching decision at a time, and decide whether that is a thing to own or a thing to unmake.

outcomes.label: Own the name, and its uses
outcomes.description: Sentiment is a luxury a long voyage cannot always afford, and the ship's hard reputation is not a shame but a tool — it opens the doors fear opens, keeps the predators at a distance, gets the ship what it needs from a dark that respects nothing else. Own it. Be, without apology, the thing the ship's choices have made, and put the fearsome name to the ship's own hard use.
outcomes.log: The council owned the hard name the ship had earned, calling it a tool rather than a stain — a reputation that opened the doors fear opens and kept the dark's predators at their distance. It was a cold self-knowledge and an unflinching one, a people looking at what its choices had made and declining to flinch even at that, and the trade-dark went on giving a wide, wary berth to a hull that had decided to be exactly as fearsome as it was.

outcomes.label: Begin the long work of a different name
outcomes.description: A reputation is only the sum of the choices so far, and the next choice is always the ship's to make. Start — knowing it will take a hundred kind acts and years of them to shift a name built by a hundred hard ones — the slow, thankless work of becoming known for something other than the cold sum. Take the merciful path now, and the next time, and the time after, and let the ledger slowly turn.
outcomes.log: The council began the long, thankless work of earning a different name — the merciful choice taken now and resolved to be taken again, against a reputation a hundred hard decisions had built and only a hundred kind ones could shift. It bought no immediate change; the dark stayed wary a long while yet. But it was a first deliberate entry against a heavy ledger, a ship deciding it would rather spend generations becoming something better than stay, efficiently, what it was.

title: The Untried Command
description: The captain is dead — no long illness, no handoff, only an empty chair by the morning watch. The heir the register names has never held a command, and every deck is watching to see what kind of hand takes the helm.

outcomes.label: Hold the old course
outcomes.description: The heir governs in continuity — the predecessor's charter and counsel kept whole.
outcomes.log: The new leader took the chair without changing a line of the standing orders. The ship steadied around a familiar hand, if an untried one.

outcomes.label: Set a new course
outcomes.description: The heir breaks with the dead and remakes the command in their own image.
outcomes.log: The heir did not wait to be measured against the dead. New orders, new faces on the council — the ship woke to a different command than it slept under.

outcomes.label: Rule by council until they are ready
outcomes.description: Hold authority in the council while the heir learns the weight of the helm.
outcomes.log: The council held the helm in trust, teaching the heir a watch at a time. Slower and safer, and it cost the ship a measure of standing to keep the peace while the chair sat half-filled.

title: The Empty Chair
description: The one who is gone led the ship for a lifetime, and the decks feel the absence like a dropped engine note. How a ship buries a captain is how it tells itself who it still is.

outcomes.label: A full ship's mourning
outcomes.description: Stand down the routine and keep the founders' rites in full; let the grief be shared.
outcomes.log: The ship stood down for the mourning and the old rites were kept whole. Grief shared is grief halved; the decks came back to work bound a little tighter.

outcomes.label: Keep the watch
outcomes.description: Bury the dead without ceremony and keep the voyage moving.
outcomes.log: There was no halt, no rite the founders would have known — a name logged, a watch kept, the voyage unbroken. Some called it strength. Some never forgave it.

title: The Long Reign
description: One hand has held the first chair longer than most captains of a ship like this ever get — long enough that a whole generation has come up knowing no other leader, long enough that the way things are done and the way this one captain does them have quietly become the same thing. It is a rare gift, an era of steady continuity. It is also a shadow the young are beginning to notice.

outcomes.label: Honor the continuity
outcomes.description: Let the long reign be what it is — a steadying hand through a stretch of the dark that needed one.
outcomes.log: The ship leaned into the long reign and let one steady hand be the thing that held it. A generation that had known no other captain took the continuity for granted, the way the lucky always do.

outcomes.label: Make room for the young
outcomes.description: Have the captain share the chair — apprentice the heirs, loosen the grip before the grip becomes the ship.
outcomes.log: At the council's urging the long-serving captain began to let others decide beside them — a deliberate loosening, so that when the reign ended the ship would not have forgotten how to steer without this one hand.

title: The Only Captain They Know
description: The leader who has held this ship for a generation is old now, and the arithmetic has begun to weigh on every deck: no one under forty has ever served under anyone else. When this reign ends — and it will — the ship will meet something it has not faced in a lifetime. The council can begin, quietly, to prepare for it, or let the good years run and trust the ending to sort itself.

outcomes.label: Prepare the succession now
outcomes.description: Name and season an heir while the captain still lives to teach them, so the handoff is a plan and not a scramble.
outcomes.log: While the old captain still had years and wits to spare them, the council named the next and set them to learn at that hand. The ship would not, this time, be caught steering blind.

outcomes.label: Let the good years run
outcomes.description: Do not borrow tomorrow's trouble. The reign is good; ride it, and face the ending when it comes.
outcomes.log: The council chose not to rush the ending of a good thing, and let the long reign run its course unhurried. The decks were the gladder for it — and a little less ready for the day the chair went cold.
